---
title: "Image Generation & Annotation"
---

::: {.topic-badges}
[Moderate]{.level-badge .level-moderate} · **Data & Analysis** track · ~35 min hands-on + readings and quiz
:::

[← Back to the Data & Analysis track](data-analysis.qmd){.track-back}

*Make communication graphics responsibly, and turn images into labeled data.*

## What you'll be able to do

- Generate communication graphics and critique them for accuracy and representation
- Annotate images (bounding boxes, COCO) for analysis
- Disclose AI-generated visuals and apply consistent labels

## Overview

Generative tools make graphics fast; annotation tools turn images into labeled data you can measure (street features, hazards, conditions). Both need judgment — generated images can mislead or misrepresent, and labels need consistent definitions.

Disclose AI-generated visuals in published materials, and check that people and places are represented fairly.

## Practice activities

```{=html}
<details class="activity"><summary>Activity 1 · Moderate — Generate and critique a safety graphic</summary>
```

**Time** ~17 min · **Tools** Adobe Firefly / Express (Drexel Moderate-Risk data)

**Goal.** Make a communication graphic responsibly.

**Setup.** No dataset needed. Open Firefly or Adobe Express.

**Steps.**

1. Generate an image:

   > Create a clean, inclusive illustration for a pedestrian-safety campaign: people of varied ages and abilities crossing safely at a marked crosswalk in a city. Flat, friendly style, with space for a headline.

2. Critique what comes back:

   - Is anything inaccurate or unsafe (e.g., people crossing against the signal)?
   - Who is represented, and who isn't?
   - What would you disclose when publishing it?

3. Regenerate to fix one issue.

**Expected result.** A usable draft graphic plus notes on accuracy, representation, and disclosure.

**Check your work.** Ask yourself: could this image mislead anyone about safe behavior? If so, fix it before using it.

**Common pitfalls.** Generated scenes can show unsafe or unrealistic situations — review carefully. Always disclose AI-generated visuals in published materials.

**Stretch (optional).** Make a second version adapted for a different neighborhood or audience.

```{=html}
</details>
```

```{=html}
<details class="activity"><summary>Activity 2 · Moderate — Annotate street images</summary>
```

**Time** ~20 min · **Tools** Label Studio (free) + a browser

**Goal.** Turn images into labeled data you could measure.

**Setup.** Use these 10 publicly available Philadelphia intersection images from Google Street View (Google automatically blurs faces and license plates). Open each link and save the image, or right-click to copy:

1. [Broad & Lehigh](https://www.google.com/maps/@39.9893,-75.1563,3a,75y,90h,90t/data=!3m6!1e1!3m4!1s0x0:0x0!2e0!7i16384!8i8192) — wide arterial, no visible crosswalk markings
2. [Broad & Walnut](https://www.google.com/maps/@39.9494,-75.1631,3a,75y,270h,90t/data=!3m6!1e1!3m4!1s0x0:0x0!2e0!7i16384!8i8192) — downtown, marked crosswalks and curb ramps
3. [Frankford & Allegheny](https://www.google.com/maps/@39.9939,-75.1234,3a,75y,180h,90t/data=!3m6!1e1!3m4!1s0x0:0x0!2e0!7i16384!8i8192) — complex intersection
4. [5th & Spring Garden](https://www.google.com/maps/@39.9619,-75.1478,3a,75y,90h,90t/data=!3m6!1e1!3m4!1s0x0:0x0!2e0!7i16384!8i8192) — near highway ramp
5. [Lancaster & 40th](https://www.google.com/maps/@39.9617,-75.2058,3a,75y,90h,90t/data=!3m6!1e1!3m4!1s0x0:0x0!2e0!7i16384!8i8192) — West Philadelphia
6. [Oregon & Broad](https://www.google.com/maps/@39.9143,-75.1631,3a,75y,270h,90t/data=!3m6!1e1!3m4!1s0x0:0x0!2e0!7i16384!8i8192) — South Philadelphia
7. [Girard & Front](https://www.google.com/maps/@39.9696,-75.1352,3a,75y,180h,90t/data=!3m6!1e1!3m4!1s0x0:0x0!2e0!7i16384!8i8192) — Fishtown
8. [Roosevelt Blvd & Adams Ave](https://www.google.com/maps/@40.0342,-75.0991,3a,75y,90h,90t/data=!3m6!1e1!3m4!1s0x0:0x0!2e0!7i16384!8i8192) — high-crash corridor
9. [Market & 52nd](https://www.google.com/maps/@39.9575,-75.2266,3a,75y,270h,90t/data=!3m6!1e1!3m4!1s0x0:0x0!2e0!7i16384!8i8192) — West Philadelphia commercial
10. [Cottman & Bustleton](https://www.google.com/maps/@40.0555,-75.0574,3a,75y,90h,90t/data=!3m6!1e1!3m4!1s0x0:0x0!2e0!7i16384!8i8192) — Northeast Philadelphia

If the Street View links don't load at the exact angle, navigate to the intersection in Google Maps and drop the Street View pegman. Install or open **Label Studio** (free, open-source).

**Steps.**

1. Set up a bounding-box project with two labels: **marked crosswalk** and **curb ramp**. Write a one-line definition for each *before* you start.

2. Label all ~10 images.

3. Export to **COCO** format.

4. Sanity-check consistency with AI:

   > Here is my labeling guide and a sample of my annotations. Point out where my label definitions are ambiguous or applied inconsistently.

**Expected result.** Ten annotated images exported as COCO, plus a tightened labeling guide.

**Check your work.** Re-label 2 images without looking at your first pass. If you disagree with yourself, the definitions need work.

**Common pitfalls.** Vague definitions wreck any downstream analysis. Never upload images with identifiable people to an unapproved tool.

**Stretch (optional).** Add a third label (e.g., "missing curb ramp") and note how it changes how consistently you can annotate.

```{=html}
</details>
```


## Check your readiness

Answer these, then check — your score suggests whether to dive in or skim the readings first.

<div class="readiness-quiz">
<script type="application/json" class="quiz-data">
{"questions": [{"q": "An AI-generated image used in a public health flyer should be…", "options": ["Checked for accuracy/representation and disclosed", "Used as-is", "Always avoided", "Never disclosed"], "answer": 0, "explain": "Review for accuracy and representation, and disclose AI-made visuals."}, {"q": "Good annotation labels need…", "options": ["Clear, consistent definitions", "Pretty colors", "A big model", "No review"], "answer": 0, "explain": "Consistent label definitions are what make annotations usable."}, {"q": "COCO is…", "options": ["A common format for image annotations", "A chatbot", "A chart type", "A statistic"], "answer": 0, "explain": "COCO is a widely used annotation/label format."}]}
</script>
</div>

## Recommended readings

Available in the shared OneDrive folder **Staff Faculty AI Workshop → Readings**, and online where linked:

- [**Anthropic Has Caught Up to OpenAI in Image Understanding**](https://www.understandingai.org/p/anthropic-has-caught-up-to-openai) — the state of multimodal image analysis.
- [**These New Tools Could Make AI Vision Systems Less Biased (MIT Tech Review)**](https://www.technologyreview.com/2023/09/25/1080191/these-new-tools-could-make-ai-vision-systems-less-biased/) — bias in computer vision and how it's addressed.

## Useful resources

- [Adobe Firefly](https://www.adobe.com/products/firefly.html) — commercially-safe image generation (Drexel Moderate-Risk data).
- [Label Studio](https://labelstud.io) — open-source image and text annotation.
- [CVAT](https://www.cvat.ai) — computer-vision annotation tool.

---

## Navigate

**Next in this track:** [AI-Assisted Web & Dashboards](web-and-dashboards.qmd)

**Related topics:** [AI for Design & Visual Communication](design-visual-communication.qmd) · [Geospatial / GIS & Built Environment](geospatial-gis.qmd)

::: {.callout-warning}
## Data rules
Don't upload images containing identifiable people or protected settings to unapproved tools; disclose AI-generated visuals. See [Tools & Access](../tools.qmd) and Drexel's [AI Tools page](https://drexel.edu/provost/ai/tools).
:::
